title: Archangel Michael, St. Gelasius, St. Karas of Egypt & St. Blaise of Sebaste
primary_saint: The Archangel Michael, St. Gelasius the Monk, St. Karas of Egypt, and St. Blaise of Sebaste
narrative:
On the twelfth of Yekatit, the monthly commemoration of the Archangel Michael fills the Church with the awareness of the invisible world that presses always upon the visible — the world of the angelic ministers who stand before the throne of God and are sent as flames of fire to serve those who will inherit salvation. Michael is in the Ethiopian tradition simultaneously the warrior who cast down Satan, the guardian of the people of Israel, the conductor of souls at the hour of death, and the monthly intercessor whose feast day gathers the faithful in every corner of the land. Churches, springs, mountains, and monasteries throughout Ethiopia bear his name, and his commemoration on this twelfth day is among the most attended liturgical occasions of the Ethiopian year. To invoke Michael is to invoke the whole hierarchy of Heaven in a single name.

Saint Gelasius the Monk — distinct from the actor-martyr Gelasius of the earlier day — is a father of the Palestinian desert tradition whose witness the Ethiopian Synaxarium preserves in connection with the virtue of obedience. The tradition records that Gelasius owned a copy of the Scriptures of great value and kept it in a place where the monks of his community could use it freely. When a visiting monk was tempted by the beauty of the codex and stole it, Gelasius discovered the theft but said nothing, choosing to bear the loss rather than shame his guest. The monk went to the city to sell the codex, found a buyer, but was suddenly overcome with compunction, returned the book, and confessed his sin; Gelasius received him with tenderness and pressed the book upon him as a gift. This story, simple as it is, encapsulates the whole pedagogy of the desert: that the abba teaches not by rule but by the practice of a love that absorbs injury without complaint.

Saint Karas of Egypt is among the most hidden figures in this month's Deep Assembly — a hermit of the Egyptian desert whose name survives in the Synaxarium with almost no biographical detail beyond the facts of his long solitude and his gift of spiritual counsel. What the tradition preserves about Karas is concentrated into a single saying: that he never allowed himself to think of a fellow monk's sin, but whenever such a thought arose in him, he immediately looked inward at his own sins until the thought of the other passed. This practice, which the desert fathers called guarding the heart, is presented as the foundation of his holiness and the explanation of why those who came to him always felt themselves loved rather than judged.

Saint Blaise of Sebaste — a bishop and physician-martyr of the same city that witnessed the martyrdom of the Forty Soldiers — brings this assembly to a close with a witness that unites healing and suffering in a single life. Blaise was bishop of Sebaste and also a healer of remarkable gifts, sought out both for physical and spiritual ailments. During the persecution he withdrew to a cave where wild animals gathered around him peaceably; soldiers sent to hunt animals for the amphitheatre found him surrounded by beasts lying quietly at his feet. He was arrested, tortured, and beheaded, but not before performing one final healing — freeing a child who was choking on a fish bone — which became the origin of the blessing of throats that the Western church performs on his feast. Michael, Gelasius, Karas, Blaise: the guardian of the heavens, the monk who absorbs injury, the hermit who guards his heart, the bishop who heals bodies — four aspects of the one ministry of protection that God exercises through His saints. Amen.
